title: Inheritance 🧬
description: Pass genes and behaviors from an overarching parent down to children.

slides.title: Repeating Logic 🤷
slides.content:
Imagine you are building a Zoo app.

You create a `Lion` class and a `Tiger` class. Both lions and tigers eat, sleep, and breathe.

Do you want to re-type the `def eat` method perfectly inside every single animal class?

slides.title: The Parent Class 👨‍👦
slides.content:
Instead of violating D.R.Y, we use **Inheritance**.

Inheritance allows a Child Class to automatically perfectly inherit all the methods and data from a broader Parent Class.

You designate the Parent using the less-than symbol `<`.

`class Child < Parent`

slides.title: The Family Tree
slides.content: Run this! Notice how `Lion` is totally empty, but it still magically has the `eat` method because it inherited it from `Animal`.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Animal
  def eat
    puts "Munch munch munch!"
  end
end

# The Lion class inherits (<) from the Animal class
class Lion < Animal
end

simba = Lion.new
simba.eat

quizData.question: Which mathematical symbol is perfectly tasked with defining class Inheritance in Ruby?
quizData.explanation: If you read `class Dog < Animal` out loud, you can think of it as 'Dog is a sub-category of Animal'.

slides.title: Is-A Relationship 🤝
slides.content:
A crucial rule of Inheritance is that it must represent an "Is-A" relationship.

A `Dog` *is an* `Animal`.
A `Car` *is a* `Vehicle`.

You should NEVER have `Dog` inherit from `Car`. That ruins your architecture!

quizData.question: If you build a massive `Vehicle` class containing a `drive` method, which of these is the most logical Child class?
quizData.explanation: A Motorcycle "is a" Vehicle. Wheels and Engines “belong to” a Vehicle, but they are not Vehicles themselves.
